body_txt: Creating an expansive character roster can be difficult, as wholly Distinctive Appearances can take a long time due to passing on pages of concept art to a management team and especially in computer graphics creating new models is labor-intensive. One cost-effective method for speeding up the process is to reuse the same model but using a different color palette. This is usually seen in some most forms of animation and video games, but it most often appears in Merchandise-Driven shows, Role Playing Games and Fighting Games . In the 8- and 16-bit era RPG s, it was pervasive: because of console limitations, disk and screen space were serious concerns. Palette Swapping was used to create a large variety of different enemies , often using different colors for various power levels . In fighting games, this is often mandatory to differentiate players using the same character by making one a different shade so they know who is who. Massively Multiplayer Online Roleplaying Games ( MMORPGs ) are often set in a very large world that must be populated by monsters. Palette Swap to the rescue! By changing the size and textures used on the same model, the designers can make many types of monsters from only a few basic meshes. Sometimes even bosses are simply re-textured and huge versions of weaker monsters. Some fans of fighting games use the term to refer to characters that use the same animations and move sets, even if the characters look very different. Such characters are also known as "clones". Individual characters may also have a choice of several different colors or costumes (or both). Beyond video games, this is a common method with any sort of animation or large-scale production design work. A more elaborate variation is the Texture Swap, where the actual model used remains the same but changing what parts of the design are highlighted can make it seem like something completely different. The Head Swap is another tried-and-true technique for making more out of less. Nearly all toylines will reuse parts with some colors changed, as much of the cost of a new toy comes from making the steel injection mold used to create parts. This can sometimes result in most of the line effectively being the same character with a different head and accessories, and is a major reason for the prevalence of the Environment-Specific Action Figure . Even in live action work, costumes may get dyed different colors and individual sections switched around to create a different profile and visual draw. A Little Black Dress and Lady in Red could be the exact same design. Sometimes characters begin as a Palette Swap of someone specific and then gain a unique personality and visual identity, which is Divergent Character Evolution . Note that Tropes Are Tools . Palette swapping may appear to just be a shortcut, but it allows designers to flesh out a character roster while keeping the designs distinctive. that keep pace with the player's progress while still hinting that the enemies will follow a familiar pattern. This is especially important when a given area is intended to be fast-paced or lead up to a climax, and the designers don't want the player stopping constantly to study "new" enemies. Also, can be used for worldbuilding: using palette-swapped monsters can hint at connections between otherwise disparate locations. In the case of bosses it can be used to hint at relationships between creatures that might not otherwise be apparent to the player. One of the best ways developers found to make this trope smarter and loved by the audience, is use it as references , usually to other aspects of a franchise . When videogames have sequels, characters can have their original clothes from previous games back. Superheroes in videogames can have multiple uniforms from their history in comics, TV shows, and movies. Characters who were completely redesigned over the years or with reboots can revert to their original designs. Characters who appeared or debuted in the early days of 3D graphics like the PlayStation 1 or the Nintendo 64 can turn into their low-poly models from those times. Even palette swaps that are just alternate colors can work as references, when they use very specific color combinations to reference other, unrelated works . Sprite Comics call this a recolor. Super-Trope to Characters Sharing a Slot and Chromatic Counterpart . Often a result of Only Six Faces . For a similar time-saving technique, see Ambidextrous Sprite . See also Colour-Coded for Your Convenience . Often associated with Moveset Clone in fighting games. Separate, but Identical is this trope for strategy games, which can also be Palette Swaps. For the level/world equivalent, see Remixed Level and Hard Mode Filler . Palette swaps can be used to make Underground Monkeys , different colors to show different elemental affinities with Color-Coded Elements , Palette-Swapped Alien Food , or just a different variant.
